label: 85_qaeda_al qaeda_zawahiri_al qaedas
description: The Brookings Institution discusses the shifting leadership of al Qaeda following the death of Osama bin Laden, including the impact on its membership and operations. It highlights key security issues and the organization's recovery from significant losses.

earliest_articles.title: @ Brookings Podcast: Holbrooke on Fighting Terrorism
earliest_articles.excerpt: The failed Christmas Day bomber may have trained in Yemen, but the nexus of al Qaeda and global terrorism remains in Afghanistan and Pakistan. In this week’s @ Brookings podcast, Richard C. Holbrooke, special U.S. representative to the region, says strengthening democracy and restoring stability in those two troubled…

earliest_articles.title: Global al Qaeda and the Christmas Terrorist
earliest_articles.excerpt: Bruce Riedel says that the nation and the president dodged a bullet when the terrorist attack on Northwest Flight 253 failed Christmas Day. The incident has highlighted flaws in our national security structure and demonstrated the increasingly global nature of al Qaeda as a terrorist organization. Despite the new…

earliest_articles.title: Go Negative
earliest_articles.excerpt: The intelligence community directors’ testimony on the imminent danger of an al Qaeda attack is only the latest depressing news in what has been a bad year for counterterrorism. The Fort Hood shootings, the near-miss airplane bombing on Christmas Day, the radicalization of parts of the Somali-American community, and…

newest_articles.title: Al-Qaida lost its leader, but are Americans any safer?
newest_articles.excerpt: The U.S. drone strike that killed al-Qaida leader Ayman al-Zawahri in Kabul last weekend jolted Americans, reminding them that Islamic extremists are still active, writes Daniel L. Byman. This op-ed originally appeared in the Los Angeles Times. The U.S. drone strike that killed al-Qaida leader Ayman al-Zawahri in…

newest_articles.title: Al-Qaida after al-Zawahri
newest_articles.excerpt: The United States’ killing of Ayman al-Zawahri, successor to Osama bin Laden as al-Qaida’s leader, marks the end of an era for the jihadi group. Any new leader will take over an organization with grand aspirations but weak capacity, Daniel Byman writes. This piece originally appeared in Lawfare. Ayman al-Zawahri, who…

newest_articles.title: The death of Ayman al-Zawahri and the future of al-Qaida
newest_articles.excerpt: Al-Qaida leader Ayman al-Zawahri may be dead or at least appears to be “completely off the grid,” according to journalist and veteran jihadi-watcher Hassan Hassan. These reports come at the same time as the killing of another very senior al-Qaida leader, Abu Muhammad al-Masri, in Tehran, reportedly by Israeli agents…

central_articles.title: Denying Terrorist Safe Havens: Homeland Security Efforts to Counter Threats from Pakistan, Yemen and Somalia
central_articles.excerpt: In testimony before the House of Representatives Committee on Homeland Security, Daniel Byman addresses key security issues, including the danger from the al Qaeda core after the death of Osama bin Laden, the impact of the Arab Spring on counterterrorism, and overall security of the U.S. homeland. I would urge this…

central_articles.title: Al Qaeda’s M&A Strategy
central_articles.excerpt: On September 11, 2006, al Qaeda celebrated the fifth anniversary of its marquee terrorist attack by announcing that it had signed up hundreds of new members — an impressive growth spurt for an organization whose membership is often estimated by American counterterrorism analysts to be in the low thousands. But al…

central_articles.title: The Al Qaeda-Iran Connection
central_articles.excerpt: As al Qaeda recovers from the SEAL raid that killed Osama bin Laden and builds a new leadership team, its inner circle has undergone a major metamorphosis, thanks to the arrival since late last year of the long- lost al Qaeda Iran cadre. After 9/11 and the toppling of the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan, a major block…
